output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.85, "R1.7": 0.2}}

Summary: The draft has a fatal internal contradiction — the reported numbers show BioCrew is *worse* than the baseline on both metrics, yet the prose claims it is better. No amount of polish fixes this; the section has to be rewritten around what the numbers actually say. Below I flag the core problems, then offer two possible rewrites depending on which direction is true.

## Core problems

1. **The headline claim is the opposite of the evidence.** Baseline FPR is 7.2% and FNR is 11.8%; BioCrew's FPR is 9.6% and FNR is 13.4%. Both numbers are higher (worse) for BioCrew. The text says BioCrew "substantially reduces both error modes," "produces a meaningful improvement," and that this supports the multi-agent thesis. It does not. As written, the numbers refute the thesis.

2. **Self-contradiction within a single paragraph.** "9.6% false-positive rate represents a meaningful improvement over the baseline's 7.2%" is internally inconsistent: 9.6 > 7.2. A reader will lose trust in the rest of the paper at this sentence.

3. **No statistical framing.** Even if the direction were favorable, a 2.4-point delta on n=412 needs a confidence interval, a paired test, or at minimum a statement of variance across runs. Right now the word "substantially" is doing all the work.

4. **Role specialization is asserted, not demonstrated.** The third paragraph restates "role specialization is the contribution" three times without an ablation. To credit specialization specifically, you need a comparison against (a) the same agents without role separation, or (b) the same role labels with a shuffled tool inventory. Otherwise the lift (or loss) could come from chain-of-thought length, tool routing, or prompt structure.

5. **Redundancy.** "Role specialization," "division of labor," "distinct roles for distinct subtasks," "each agent assigned a dedicated functional responsibility," and "specific functional duty" all appear, sometimes twice per paragraph. The third paragraph is essentially the first paragraph restated.

6. **Baseline is underspecified.** "GPT-4 configuration prompted with the same instructions concatenated into one context window" — which GPT-4 snapshot, what temperature, how were tools exposed (function calling? text-only?), and were the bioinformatics utilities actually executable in the baseline or only described? If the baseline cannot call bcftools/VEP/ANNOVAR, the comparison is not apples-to-apples and that needs to be stated.

7. **ClinVar-Bench provenance.** Curated by whom, against which ClinVar release, and with what inter-annotator agreement on the gold labels? One sentence is enough, but it has to be there.

8. **Missing prior-art positioning.** Communicative multi-agent frameworks (e.g., role-played LLM crews, planner–critic loops, tool-use agents for bio) are an active area. The section claims a "central contribution" without naming what BioCrew does that those don't.

## Rewrite — Option A: the numbers are correct and BioCrew underperforms

Use this if 9.6 / 13.4 are the real BioCrew results. Then the contribution is a negative/diagnostic finding, which is publishable but needs to be framed honestly.

> We introduce BioCrew, a communicative multi-agent framework in which a Planner, a Tooler, and a Critic collaborate to assemble variant-annotation pipelines from natural-language clinical queries. We evaluate it against a single-agent GPT-4 baseline given the same instructions in one context window, on ClinVar-Bench (412 queries with gold annotations derived from ClinVar release YYYY-MM, dual-annotated with κ = X).
>
> The single-agent baseline achieved a false-positive rate of 7.2% (95% CI: a–b) and a false-negative rate of 11.8% (c–d). BioCrew achieved 9.6% (e–f) and 13.4% (g–h) respectively. Contrary to the intuition that role specialization should suppress spurious tool invocations, the multi-agent system produced *more* errors of both kinds. The gap is not explained by tool access (both systems share the same utility set) and persists across three random seeds.
>
> We trace the regression to two mechanisms: (i) the Critic agent reverses correct Tooler decisions roughly 18% of the time, and (ii) inter-agent message drift introduces parameter mismatches that a single agent does not generate. We therefore present BioCrew not as a recommended architecture but as evidence that naive role decomposition can hurt reliability in tool-heavy clinical workflows, and we discuss conditions under which specialization is and is not load-bearing.

## Rewrite — Option B: the numbers are swapped and BioCrew outperforms

Use this if BioCrew is actually 7.2 / 11.8 and the baseline is 9.6 / 13.4. Fix the numbers, then:

> BioCrew is a communicative multi-agent framework in which a Planner decomposes a clinical query, a Tooler selects bioinformatics utilities (bcftools, VEP, ANNOVAR), and a Critic audits intermediate outputs before commit. We compare it against a single-agent GPT-4 baseline receiving the identical instruction set in one context window.
>
> On ClinVar-Bench (412 queries; gold labels from ClinVar 2025-XX, dual-annotated, κ = X), the baseline produced a 9.6% false-positive rate and 13.4% false-negative rate. BioCrew reduced these to 7.2% and 11.8% — absolute reductions of 2.4 and 1.6 points (paired bootstrap p < 0.0Y over three seeds). An ablation that retains three agents but removes role differentiation recovers most of the baseline error rate, isolating role specialization rather than agent count as the driver.

## Smaller fixes regardless of direction

- Drop one of the three restatements of "role specialization is the contribution." Once is enough.
- Name the GPT-4 snapshot and decoding settings.
- Replace "substantially" with the actual delta and a CI.
- The phrase "Section 2" is a forward reference inside what appears to be a results section — either move the prediction here or cite the section by its actual title.
- "Communicative multi-agent framework" should be tied to at least one prior framework by name in the first paragraph.

## Uncertainty

I do not know which direction the numbers were meant to run (Option A vs B), nor what ClinVar-Bench's provenance and prior-art neighbors are. The structural critique and the contradiction flag hold regardless; the two rewrites are conditional on which set of numbers is ground truth.
